lesson_objective: A method lets you name a block of logic once and reuse it by calling that name, instead of retyping the same code everywhere you need it.
lesson_summary: This lesson contrasts a program with duplicated logic scattered across three places against the same program refactored into one named method called three times, then breaks down a method's signature.
key_points: Duplicated logic means duplicated bugs — fix it in one place, forget the other two.
A method bundles logic under one name you can call from anywhere.
A method's signature (name, parameters, return type) is the contract for how it's used.
step_by_step_explanation: Show the same three-line calculation duplicated in three places in a program.
Extract it into one method with a clear name and a return type.
Call the method three times instead, producing identical output with a fraction of the code.
Label the method signature on screen: name, parameter list, return type — the three things a caller needs to know.
Land on the takeaway: a method is a promise — call it the same way, get the same kind of result, every time.

core_example.description: The same 'square a number' calculation duplicated three times, refactored into a single reusable method.
core_example.focus_note: Highlight the method signature line (static int square(int n)) when explaining name, parameter, and return type.

analogy: A method is like saving a phone number under a contact name — you don't have to remember or retype the number every time, you just call the name.
common_mistakes: Forgetting to specify a return type, or using void when a value actually needs to come back.
Confusing defining a method with calling it — writing the method body doesn't run it.
what_to_avoid_saying: Do not say a method 'runs automatically' once defined — it only executes when explicitly called.
beginner_takeaway: If you're about to type the same logic a second time, that's the method calling your name, not the other way around.
retention_hook: This program has the same three lines of math typed out three separate times. Here's the one-method fix.
visual_notes: Show the three duplicated code blocks highlighted in matching colors before the refactor.
Label each part of the method signature (name / parameters / return type) as callouts on screen.
